rewritten_text: **Alex Hormozi** (Gym Launch founder): A 22-year-old pizza shop operator scaled one location to $2.4M revenue with 30% margins; before expanding to a second location, he prioritized installing a general manager to run the original store autonomously, ensuring the business could operate without founder involvement — a prerequisite for multi-location growth.
notes: Unresolved "founder" reference and isolated numbers (30%, $2.4M) without axis context.

rewritten_text: B2B businesses with 80%+ annual retention (20% maximum churn) achieve 2.5x higher customer lifetime value compared to 50% retention, enabling more aggressive acquisition spend. Example: $100/year customer at 50% retention = ~$200 LTV; same customer at 80% retention = ~$500 LTV, all else equal. Mechanism: high retention allows arbitrage on CAC and eliminates constant customer-replacement costs.
notes: Numbers lack proper axis framing; generic role references (new practitioner) unresolved. Claims lack source attribution in rendered text.

notes: Edge text is feature-list non-claim (A2) with no load-bearing assertion; vague blanket (A4) without operationalizable mechanism. "Get paid when x happens" is not falsifiable without specifying X.

rewritten_text: Service business pricing: founders should raise prices consistently as demand exceeds supply; margin pressure decreases when reputation and supply-side constraint grow. SaaS: gross margins reach 70-90% post-PMF but consumer monetization is constrained ($9-13/mo); enterprise SaaS commands $100k+/year. E-commerce: margin reinvestment into inventory is non-optional for growth; arbitrage (buy $10, sell $11) leaves zero margin for brand.
notes: Generic references ('founder', 'the buyer') unresolved in source context; needs named speaker attribution.

node_id: edge:Close Rate Analysis And Price Optimization|Sales Process Optimization
rewritten_text: When sales close rate is 80%+ on a given offer, price can typically increase 3-4x before conversion drops to historical baseline; lower close rates indicate pricing below market-clearing level and signal optimization opportunity.
notes: Decontextualized number (3-4x) with no comparison axis; vague comparative (optimal pricing). Needs conditions and mechanism.
Clear summary; resolvable entity (Hormozi by context); standalone meaning; source-grounded.

rewritten_text: A skincare company at $4.5M monthly revenue ($54M ARR) targeting $25M annual revenue requires top-layer leadership hires; CEO involvement in key hiring (C-suite, VP roles) differs by function, with network strategy (internal vs. external sourcing) varying by role criticality.
notes: Generic actor reference ('the CEO'); decontextualized numbers ($4.5M, $25M) without growth axis or time frame.

rewritten_text: Price tier jumps should be 5-10x, not 50% increments, to create market segmentation. Example: product at $1K tier; next tier should be $5K-$10K (not $1.5K), signaling material upgrade and justifying feature/service differentiation. Mechanism: large jumps allow 80% of buyers to stay in lower tier (higher volume) while top 20% ascend to premium tier with better unit economics.
notes: Generic 'the buyer' reference; needs speaker attribution (appears to be Hormozi from source file).
Clear, resolvable summary; standalone meaning; source-grounded.

rewritten_text: Founder-concentrated single-location business maintains higher margins than multi-location operations due to founder leverage; geographic expansion requires brand premium (higher prices or superior perceived quality) to offset coordination and communication costs, or unit economics erode.
notes: Vague comparative (superior margins) without metric; generic reference (founder). Needs quantified axis (margin % at 1 vs. 2+ locations).

rewritten_text: **Video Sales Letter (VSL) Framework** — a 5-7 minute presale format used by B2B service and physical product sellers. Structure: (1) Hook — specific benefit + ROI quantifier (e.g., 'railings cost 5% of resale value added'); (2) Promise — outcome the buyer gets; (3) Proof — 2-3 named customer examples with before/after; (4) Plan — 3-4 steps buyer takes; (5) Pricing — transparent tier ladder + objection pre-handling; (6) CTA — book call or watch testimonial. Mechanism: VSL qualifies high-intent buyers before sales calls, reducing no-shows and increasing close rate.
notes: Missing speaker attribution (appears to be Hormozi); example numbers (railings = 'basically free') lack axis context.
